title: Module C: Cryptography
summary: Cryptography turns readable data into something unreadable to anyone without the right key, and provides the building blocks for confidentiality, integrity, and data authentication. This lesson walks from randomness and the historical Caesar cipher through symmetric and public-key crypto, hashing, MACs and authenticated encryption, RSA/Diffie-Hellman/ECC, key derivation, the quantum threat and post-quantum cryptography, digital signatures, certificates/PKI, and signed electronic documents (formats, multiple signatures, time-stamping, qualified signatures).
objectives: State the security properties cryptography supports: confidentiality, integrity, and data authentication.
Distinguish true randomness from pseudo-randomness and explain the role of the seed, entropy, and why randomness matters for keys and ciphertext.
Explain Kerckhoffs' principle and why 'security through obscurity' fails.
Distinguish symmetric from asymmetric (public-key) cryptography and know when each is used.
Recognise the Caesar cipher as the first known encryption algorithm and explain why it is weak and obsolete.
Compute brute-force cost from key length and explain why algorithms become obsolete.
Describe stream vs. block ciphers and the modes of operation (ECB, CBC, CFB, OFB, CTR) with their formulas and padding.
Describe hashing, MACs/HMAC, and authenticated encryption (AEAD/GCM), and what each guarantees.
Reproduce the RSA key-generation and encryption/decryption equations and the Diffie-Hellman key agreement, including the man-in-the-middle attack.
Explain ECC, key derivation functions (PBKDF2, HKDF), and the quantum threat (Shor, Grover) and post-quantum cryptography.
Explain at a high level how certificates and a PKI bind a public key to an identity, including revocation (CRL/OCSP).
Describe signed-document formats (enveloping/enveloped/detached), multiple signatures, time-stamping, and qualified electronic signatures.

blocks.text: What cryptography is for

Cryptography is the art of transforming a **message** so that it is protected even if it falls into the wrong hands. The original readable message is the **plaintext**; the scrambled output is the **ciphertext**. A **cipher** turns plaintext into ciphertext using a **key**; a **decipher** step reverses it.

The transformation is controlled by keys, not just by the algorithm:

- $C = enc(K, P)$ encrypts plaintext $P$ with key $K$ to get ciphertext $C$.
- $P = dec(K, C)$ recovers the plaintext.

Across this lesson the slides use cryptography to achieve three properties:

- **Confidentiality** — only authorized parties can read the data (achieved by encryption).
- **Integrity** — the data has not been altered (achieved by hashes/digests).
- **(Data) authentication** — the data really comes from who it claims (achieved by MACs, with a shared secret, and by digital signatures, with a private key).

blocks.title: Integrity vs. authentication
blocks.text: A plain hash proves **integrity** (nothing changed) but not **authentication** (who sent it). To get authentication you must mix in a **secret** — a shared key (MAC) or a private key (signature). Keep this distinction in mind; it is a frequent exam point.
Randomness and pseudo-randomness

Cryptography depends heavily on randomness, and the slides distinguish two sources.

- **True random numbers/sequences** are generated by genuinely **random physical processes** — white noise, Brownian motion, and similar. They cannot be predicted.
- **Pseudo-random numbers/sequences** are produced by a **deterministic** process that is statistically indistinguishable from true randomness. They need **sources of entropy** to start. If the underlying process is weak, the sequence becomes **predictable**.

A pseudo-random generator can only ever produce a **finite number** of distinct sequences. Imagine those sequences numbered and each associated with an integer — that integer is the **seed**. Feeding the same seed into the generator always reproduces exactly the **same sequence**. This is precisely why a symmetric stream cipher can be reproduced by the receiver: the key is used as the seed.

blocks.title: Why randomness matters
blocks.text:
Randomness is needed for two different reasons:

- **Process (the input):** you must select a random number and keep it secret — e.g. a cryptographic key. If an attacker can predict the key, or even part of it, guessing the rest becomes easy.
- **Effect (the output):** the result of a cryptographic operation must look random, so attackers cannot extract any knowledge from the ciphertext.
Kerckhoffs' principle and 'security through obscurity'

**Security through obscurity (STO)** is the belief that something is secure simply because how it works is kept secret. This is an illusion: it was already declared useless by Hobbs in 1851. As Shannon put it, "The enemy knows the system."

If the keys are (1) kept secret, (2) managed only by trusted systems, and (3) of adequate length, then it does **not** matter whether the encryption/decryption algorithms are secret.

In fact it is better to make the algorithms public, so the whole world can analyse them and find weaknesses. Security must rest on the **key**, never on the secrecy of the algorithm.

blocks.title: Common misconception
blocks.text: "A secret, custom algorithm is safer than a public one." Wrong. Public, well-scrutinised algorithms (AES, SHA-2) are trusted because they have survived years of public attack. A hidden algorithm is just an unaudited algorithm.
The Caesar cipher

The **Caesar cipher** is the first known encryption algorithm. It associates every letter with another one selected next in the alphabetical order — that is, each letter is replaced by another a fixed number of positions later in the alphabet.

It is **weak and obsolete** — interesting only historically.

blocks.title: Why we still mention it
blocks.text: The Caesar cipher is the historical starting point of cryptography. The slides keep it only as a curiosity: it is weak and obsolete, and serves to introduce the idea that real security must come from a strong, secret key rather than from a fixed, public rule.
Symmetric cryptography

The **same key** is shared by sender and receiver and used for both encryption and decryption: $C = enc(K, P)$ and $P = dec(K, C)$. It has **low computational cost**, making it the best choice for encrypting **large quantities of data**.

The central problem: **how do the two parties share the key securely** in the first place?

blocks.caption: Brute-force cost: an n-bit key requires up to 2^n trials. Each tried key costs the time to run the decryption PLUS the time to evaluate whether the guess was correct.

blocks.text:
The strength of a symmetric algorithm (assuming it is well designed) is the **time needed to perform all the trials** compared to the time the attacker can afford — it is **context dependent** (hours, days, years), entirely relative to the attacker's resources. For the same algorithm, **longer keys are stronger**. The numbers grow explosively:

- $2^{40} = 1\,099\,511\,627\,776$ (about $10^{12}$).
- $2^{256}$ is a 78-digit number — astronomically large.

- Old **DES** used a 56-bit key and is now obsolete.
- Modern keys should be at least **128 bits**. **AES** (128/192/256-bit) is the standard.

blocks.title: Strength is relative, not absolute
blocks.text: The slides stress that 'strong enough' is context dependent: the same algorithm may be considered safe for hours, days, or years depending on how much computing power the attacker can throw at it. And the cost per tried key is not just the decryption itself — you must also account for the **time to evaluate** whether each guessed key was correct. Both timings together determine how long a brute-force attack really takes.

Two forces shrink the effective key space over time:

- **Technology evolution** — computers run far more trials per unit time, and brute force is **trivially parallelisable** (e.g. GPUs with hundreds of cores).
- **Cryptanalysis** — theoretical and statistical techniques that, based on observations, **reduce the search space**. For DES, cryptanalysis cut the effective strength from **56 → ~40 bits**.

Even a properly sized key ($\geq 128$ bit) must eventually be **refreshed**: for time reasons (the attacker has had time to test many keys) and use reasons (the more data encrypted under a key, the more a cryptanalyst learns). When you renew a key, choose it **randomly** — there is no need to brute-force a key you can guess or predict.

The slides list the main symmetric algorithms:

- **AES** — AES-128, AES-192, AES-256 (the modern standard).
- **DES** and **3DES** (triple-DES) — obsolete / legacy.
- **RC2, RC4**.
- **ChaCha20** (and its predecessor **Salsa20**).
- **ARIA, SEED, SM4, CAMELLIA, CAST** — national / alternative block ciphers.

Bitwise **XOR** ($\oplus$) is the ideal confusion operator and a primitive available on every CPU. Its key property: if one input is random (probability $0:1 = 50:50\%$), then the **output is also equally random**, regardless of the other input. Truth table:

- $0 \oplus 0 = 0$
- $0 \oplus 1 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 0 = 1$
- $1 \oplus 1 = 0$

This is why stream ciphers XOR a pseudo-random keystream with the plaintext, and why CBC XORs each plaintext block with the previous ciphertext block.

Symmetric security rests on key length. If the algorithm is well designed and the $n$-bit key is secret, the only attack left is **brute force** — trying every key — requiring up to $T = 2^n$ trials.

- Old **DES** used a 56-bit key and is now obsolete; cryptanalysis even cut its effective strength below 56 bits.
- Modern keys should be at least **128 bits** ($2^{128}$ is astronomically large). **AES** (128/192/256-bit) is the standard.

Algorithms still become obsolete over time as hardware (e.g. GPUs with hundreds of cores) and cryptanalysis shrink the effective search space, so keys must be refreshed and chosen **randomly**.

- **Stream ciphers** process data one bit or byte at a time. A pseudo-random generator, seeded by the key, produces a keystream that is **XORed** with the plaintext. Examples: RC4 (old), Salsa20, ChaCha20 (state of the art). The ideal but impractical case is the **one-time pad** (key as long as the message).
- **Block ciphers** transform fixed-size blocks (64-bit = obsolete, 128-bit = modern). The key decides the exact transformation. AES is a block cipher.

A block-cipher algorithm only tells you how to manipulate a **single** block. **Modes of operation** apply it to real data, in three situations:

- **More data than one block:** **ECB** (Electronic Code Book), **CBC** (Cipher Block Chaining).
- **Less data than one block:** **padding** and **cipher stealing**; also simulating stream ciphers with a block cipher via **CFB** (Cipher FeedBack) and **OFB** (Output FeedBack).
- **Cipher data and number the blocks:** **CTR** (Counter mode).

Each block is encrypted independently with the key: $C_i = enc(K, P_i)$.

ECB must **NOT** be used on long messages because:

- **swapping** two ciphertext blocks goes **undetected**;
- **identical plaintext blocks produce identical ciphertext blocks**, leaking patterns and making it vulnerable to **known-plaintext attacks** (the classic example is the still-recognisable encrypted image).

blocks.caption: ECB mode: each block encrypted independently — insecure for long messages.

blocks.text:
Each plaintext block is XORed with the **previous ciphertext block** before encryption, so identical plaintext blocks no longer give identical ciphertext:

$C_i = enc(K,\; P_i \oplus C_{i-1})$.

It requires a starting value $C_0 = IV$, the **Initialization Vector**.

blocks.tex: C_i = enc(K,\; P_i \oplus C_{i-1}), \qquad C_0 = IV
blocks.caption: CBC mode: chain each block with the previous ciphertext; needs an Initialization Vector.

blocks.text:
A block cipher needs the data length to be a multiple of the block size $B$. If the data $D$ is not a multiple of $B$, **padding** adds bits until the next multiple of $B$ is reached, forming $N$ full blocks.

Problems with padding:

- you must **transmit/store more data** ($B$-aligned) than the real payload ($D$);
- you must decide the **value of the padding bits** (a careless scheme can enable attacks).

blocks.title: Never use ECB on real data
blocks.text: ECB leaks structure: equal plaintext blocks → equal ciphertext blocks, and blocks can be reordered undetected. Use a chaining/counter mode (CBC, CTR) or, better, an authenticated mode (GCM).

blocks.title: Modes of operation
blocks.text: A block cipher only defines how to handle one block. **Modes of operation** extend it to longer messages: **ECB** (insecure — identical plaintext blocks give identical ciphertext blocks, leaking patterns), **CBC** (chains each block with the previous via XOR, needs an Initialization Vector), and **CTR**, CFB, OFB. **Never use ECB on real data.**
Hashing and message digests

**Integrity check — high-level idea:** the sender runs a function over the data while sure it is correct, and sends both the data and this evidence. The recipient runs the **same function** on the data received and **compares**: equal results mean the data are intact; otherwise integrity is violated. The comparison only works if the evidence is exchanged using secure algorithms and in secure ways.

A **hash function** produces a fixed-length **digest** (a "summary") of a message of any length. A good hash must be:

- **fast** to compute,
- **one-way** — practically impossible to invert,
- **collision-resistant** — hard to find two messages with the same digest.

Ideal behaviour: changing **one bit** of input flips about **50%** of the digest bits (the avalanche effect). Examples: MD5 (broken), SHA-1 (deprecated), **SHA-256 / SHA-2** (good), **SHA-3 / Keccak** (excellent).

blocks.term: Digest length and bits of security
blocks.text:
It is always possible to find messages with the same digest; the cost depends on the digest size $n$:

- to find a message with the **same digest as a fixed message**: about $2^{n}$ attempts (a preimage/second-preimage search);
- to find **any two** messages with the same digest: about $2^{n/2}$ attempts (a collision, cheaper because of the birthday paradox).

A hash is said to have **$n$ bits of security** if the easiest attack needs on average $2^{n}$ attempts. A hash function is considered **valid only if it has a security strength of at least 80 bits**. Hence digests need many bits to make collisions statistically negligible.

From the slides' table (block size, digest size, standard, status):

- **MD2** — 8-bit block, 128-bit digest, RFC-1319 — obsolete.
- **MD4** — 512-bit block, 128-bit digest, RFC-1320 — obsolete.
- **MD5** — 512-bit block, 128-bit digest, RFC-1321 — obsolete.
- **RIPEMD-160** — 512-bit block, 160-bit digest, ISO/IEC 10118-3 — good?
- **SHA-1** — 512-bit block, 160-bit digest, FIPS 180-1 — maybe (deprecated).
- **SHA-224 / SHA-256** — 512-bit block, 224/256-bit digest (FIPS 180-2) — good.
- **SHA-384 / SHA-512** — 512-bit block, 384/512-bit digest (FIPS 180-3). These four make up the **SHA-2** family.
- **SHA-3** — block 1152–576, digest 224–512, FIPS 202 (also FIPS 180-4) — excellent.

When a serious attack on **SHA-1** appeared, a quick remediation was needed:

- **SHA-2** = {SHA-224, SHA-256, SHA-384, SHA-512}, built on the **same math as SHA-1**. SHA-256 uses a larger internal word (32-bit), SHA-512 uses 64-bit words; **SHA-224 and SHA-384 are truncations** of SHA-256 and SHA-512 respectively.
- **SHA-3** was selected through an open **competition**; it is a **completely new scheme**, won by **Keccak** (pronounced "catch-ack"). It is the **best one available nowadays**.

blocks.title: The birthday paradox in digests
blocks.text: For an $n$-bit digest, finding a message matching a given digest takes about $2^{n}$ tries, but finding any two messages that collide takes only about $2^{n/2}$ tries. That is why digests need many bits: a 256-bit hash gives only ~128 bits of collision security. Underestimating this is a classic mistake.

You **prove you are a specific user** by demonstrating that you know a **secret** that only you can know (or that you share with the other parties in the authentication). In cryptography, **keys are kept secret**, so they can be used for **data authentication**: you send a digest computed not only over the data but **also over a secret (key)** — a **keyed digest**, also called a **Message Authentication Code (MAC)**.

A bare hash protects integrity only against accidental errors — **not against an active attacker**. In a man-in-the-middle scenario the attacker can change the data and recompute the hash, since the hash function is public. To stop this you must add a secret.

A **Message Authentication Code (MAC)**, or **keyed digest**, is a digest computed over both the data **and a shared secret key**. Only someone holding the key can produce or verify it, so a matching MAC proves both **integrity and authentication**. The standard construction is **HMAC**:

$$hmac = H\big(K' \oplus opad \;\|\; H(K' \oplus ipad \;\|\; data)\big)$$

When one operation must provide confidentiality and authentication together, use **Authenticated Encryption (AE/AEAD)**, e.g. **GCM** (used by TLS), which outputs ciphertext plus an authentication **tag**.

blocks.caption: HMAC (RFC-2104): ipad = 0x36 repeated B times, opad = 0x5C repeated B times.

blocks.text:
**Authenticated Encryption** provides **privacy and authentication (and integrity) in one single operation** — one key, one algorithm. Advantages: better speed and less chance of error than combining encryption and a MAC by hand. It is needed by real applications (e-mail messages, network packets).

- **GCM** is the most used AE algorithm, used by **TLS**; during encryption it produces **ciphertext + an authentication tag**, and is fast on current CPUs.
- **AEAD (Authenticated Encryption with Associated Data)** also authenticates extra **associated data** (e.g. a packet **header**) that is not encrypted but is integrity-protected. Inputs are a **key**, a **nonce**, the associated data, and the plaintext payload; the output is the header, the nonce, the encrypted `{payload}`, and a **MAC named TAG** (which may be hidden inside the encrypted payload).

question.prompt: Alice sends Bob a file plus its SHA-256 hash over an open network. An active attacker sits in the middle. What does the hash alone guarantee?

options.content: Confidentiality — the attacker cannot read the file.
Authentication — Bob is sure the file came from Alice.
Nothing against an active attacker, who can alter the file and recompute the hash.
Integrity that survives even active tampering of the data.

question.explanation: C is correct: because the hash function is public and keyless, an active man-in-the-middle can replace the file and its hash, so the bare hash gives no protection against tampering. A is wrong — hashing does not encrypt, so confidentiality is not provided. B requires a secret (a MAC), which a plain hash lacks. D is wrong — a keyless hash only catches accidental changes, not deliberate tampering, since the attacker can recompute it.
question.theory: A plain hash gives integrity only against accidents; to authenticate against an active attacker you must add a secret (MAC or signature).

question.prompt: A hash produces an $n$-bit digest. Roughly how many attempts are needed to find ANY two messages that share a digest (a collision)?

options.content: About $2^{n}$ attempts, the same as inverting the hash.
About $2^{n/2}$ attempts, far fewer, because of the birthday paradox.
About $n$ attempts, linear in the digest size.
It is mathematically impossible to find any collision.

question.explanation: B is correct: finding any colliding pair needs only about $2^{n/2}$ tries (birthday paradox), which is why a 256-bit hash gives only ~128 bits of collision security. A is the cost of the harder task of matching a fixed digest (preimage). C wildly underestimates the work. D is wrong — collisions always exist because inputs are unbounded but digests are fixed-length; they are merely hard to find.
question.theory: Collision cost is 2^(n/2), not 2^n, so an n-bit digest gives only n/2 bits of collision security.

blocks.text: Asymmetric (public-key) cryptography

Symmetric crypto has a scaling problem: for $N$ people to communicate privately in pairs you need $N \times (N-1)/2$ keys, all distributed securely out-of-band. Public-key cryptography solves both the key-sharing and the signing problems.

blocks.caption: Number of distinct symmetric keys needed for N users to communicate pairwise.

blocks.text:
Keys are generated in **pairs** with inverse functionality: what one key does, only its partner can undo. They are not random — they share a mathematical relationship.

- The **private key** is kept secret by the owner.
- The **public key** is distributed to everyone (no confidentiality needed).

Public-key algorithms are **computationally heavy**, so they are used for small amounts of data — distributing a symmetric key, or signing a digest. Main algorithms: **Diffie-Hellman, RSA, DSA, ElGamal, ECDSA**.

Public-key crypto can produce a **secret message for a particular receiver given only its public key** — no pre-shared secret is required. The sender encrypts with the receiver's **public key**; only the receiver, holding the matching **private key**, can decrypt and read the **private message**. This is the standalone counterpart of data authentication (which instead encrypts with the sender's private key), and it is what makes the hybrid 'enveloped data' scheme below possible.

- **RSA (Rivest-Shamir-Adleman):** based on the **product of prime numbers** (inverse problem: **factoring** the result); works in **finite fields (modulo $n$)**; supports both **secrecy and digital signature**.
- **DSA (Digital Signature Algorithm):** based on **computing the power** (inverse problem: **discrete logarithm**); **only for digital signatures** — for encryption it must be combined with **ElGamal**.

- Public **module** $n = p\,q$, known to anybody, where $p$ and $q$ are **prime, large, and secret**.
- Compute $\phi = (p-1)(q-1)$.
- Choose a **public exponent** $e$ with $1 < e < \phi$, relatively prime to $\phi$.
- Compute the **private exponent** $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi$.

**Public key** $= (n, e)$; **private key** $= (n, d)$.

If an attacker learns $p$ and $q$ he can compute $d$; you can also compute $d$ from $n$ and $e$ (which is exactly the hard part for the attacker — see below).

RSA can cipher/decipher only data whose value is **less than the module $n$** ($P < n$):

- encrypt: $C = P^{e} \bmod n$;
- decrypt: $P = C^{d} \bmod n$.

The roles of $e$ and $d$ are **interchangeable** because $(P^{d})^{e} \bmod n = (P^{e})^{d} \bmod n$. The cost of the operation depends on the number of **1-bits** in the exponent.

blocks.tex: C = P^{e} \bmod n \qquad\qquad P = C^{d} \bmod n
blocks.caption: RSA encryption and decryption (requires P < n).

blocks.text:
Two related hard problems underlie RSA's security:

- **RSA problem:** given the public $(n, e)$, compute $d$.
- **Factorization problem:** given $(n, e)$, find the primes $p$ and $q$ such that $n = p q$; having factored $n$, computing $d$ is then easy.

**Factorization is the hardest** of the two: even if you somehow know both $e$ and $d$, you still cannot factor $n$.

Estimated attack times (without quantum computers):

- **512-bit** keys can be attacked in **weeks**.
- **1024-bit** keys can be attacked in **months**.
- **2048-bit** keys offer an appropriate security level for **several years**.

blocks.title: Two ways to use a key pair

steps.label: Confidentiality
steps.text: Encrypt with the **recipient's public key**. Only the recipient's private key can decrypt, so only they can read it.

steps.label: Authentication / signing
steps.text: Encrypt (sign) with the **sender's private key**. Anyone can verify with the sender's public key, which proves it came from the sender and guarantees data authentication and integrity.

steps.label: Hybrid encryption (the practical pattern)
steps.text: Generate a random symmetric key $K$, encrypt the bulk message with fast symmetric crypto using $K$, then encrypt only $K$ with the recipient's public key. This 'enveloped data' combines speed with easy key distribution.

blocks.title: RSA in a nutshell
blocks.text:
RSA relies on the difficulty of **factoring** large numbers.

- Pick two large secret primes $p, q$; the public modulus is $n = pq$.
- Compute $\phi = (p-1)(q-1)$, choose a public exponent $e$, and derive the private exponent $d = e^{-1} \bmod \phi$.
- **Public key** = $(n, e)$, **private key** = $(n, d)$.
- Encrypt: $C = P^{e} \bmod n$. Decrypt: $P = C^{d} \bmod n$.

Breaking RSA means factoring $n$ back into $p$ and $q$ — infeasible for large $n$. A 2048-bit modulus is considered secure for years. **Diffie-Hellman** instead lets two parties agree on a shared secret over a public channel (key agreement), and **ECC** achieves the same security with much shorter keys (~1/10).

blocks.title: Hybrid encryption: key distribution + bulk encryption

steps.label: Pick a message key
steps.text: Randomly generate a symmetric **message key $K$** and encrypt the actual message with a fast **symmetric** algorithm using $K$.

steps.label: Wrap the key
steps.text: Encrypt only the small key $K$ with an **asymmetric** algorithm (e.g. RSA) using the **recipient's public key** $K_{Y,pub}$ — the asymmetric encrypt step the slide labels $\varepsilon$.

steps.label: Send 'enveloped data'
steps.text: Send the symmetric ciphertext together with the RSA-encrypted $K$. The recipient applies the asymmetric decrypt step (labelled $\delta$ on the slide) with their private key $K_{Y,pri}$ to recover $K$, then decrypts the bulk message with $K$. This combines symmetric **speed** with asymmetric **easy key distribution**.

blocks.text: Diffie-Hellman key agreement

The **first public-key algorithm** ever invented; it is used to **agree on a secret key** (key agreement), not to encrypt. With public parameters $p > g > 1$:

- $X$ picks a secret $X$ and sends $A = g^{X} \bmod p$; $Y$ picks a secret $Y$ and sends $B = g^{Y} \bmod p$.
- $X$ computes $K_A = B^{X} \bmod p$; $Y$ computes $K_B = A^{Y} \bmod p$.
- Both arrive at the same shared secret: $K_A = K_B = g^{XY} \bmod p = K_{AB}$.

DH **resists a sniffing (passive) attacker**, who sees only $A$ and $B$ but cannot derive $g^{XY}$.

blocks.tex: K_A = B^{X} \bmod p = A^{Y} \bmod p = g^{XY} \bmod p = K_{AB}
blocks.caption: Diffie-Hellman shared secret, with public p > g > 1 and secret exponents X, Y.

blocks.title: DH man-in-the-middle attack
blocks.text: DH resists passive sniffing, but if an attacker $Z$ can **manipulate** the traffic, a **man-in-the-middle** attack is possible: $Z$ replaces both exchanged values with $M = g^{Z} \bmod p$. Then $X$ shares $g^{XZ} \bmod p$ with $Z$ and $Y$ shares $g^{YZ} \bmod p$ with $Z$ — two different keys, both known to $Z$, while $X$ and $Y$ believe they share one. Therefore DH **requires pre-authentication** to be safe in the wild.

blocks.title: Quantum threat
blocks.text: **Shor's algorithm** on a large quantum computer would factor integers (and solve discrete logs) efficiently, breaking RSA, DSA, and ECC. Such machines do not yet exist, but 'harvest now, decrypt later' is a real worry, driving **post-quantum cryptography** (NIST's ML-KEM, ML-DSA, SLH-DSA). Symmetric crypto and hashes are largely safe — Grover's algorithm only halves their strength, fixed by doubling the key length.
Digital signatures

To sign efficiently, the signer hashes the message and encrypts **the digest** with their **private key**:

- Signer $S$: $H = enc(S_{Kpri}, hash(M))$, then sends $M \;\|\; H$.
- Verifier $V$: $X = dec(S_{Kpub}, H)$; if $X == hash(M)$ then OK, else ALARM.

This ensures **data authentication and integrity** together (both or none) — only the holder of the private key could have produced it, and anyone with the public key can verify it. Padding must also be managed to avoid attacks.

question.prompt: In RSA, which key does Alice use to create a digital signature on a document, and which key does Bob use to verify it?

options.content: Alice signs with Bob's public key; Bob verifies with his own private key.
Alice signs with her private key; Bob verifies with Alice's public key.
Alice signs with her public key; Bob verifies with Alice's private key.
Both use a single shared symmetric key for it.

question.explanation: B is correct: signing uses the signer's private key (only Alice has it, giving data authentication and integrity), and verification uses Alice's public key (everyone has it). A describes encryption for confidentiality, not signing, and mixes up the owners. C reverses the secrecy — the private key can never be public. D describes a MAC, which gives authentication with a shared secret but is not a digital signature, since both parties hold the same key.
question.theory: Sign with your private key, verify with the signer's public key — the reverse of encryption for confidentiality.

blocks.text: Elliptic Curve Cryptography (ECC)

Instead of modular arithmetic, ECC performs operations on a **2-D elliptic curve** (e.g. $y^2 = x^3 - x$). Point addition $R = P + Q$ is defined geometrically: draw the line through $P$ and $Q$, find the third intersection $-P-Q$ with the curve, and reflect it. The underlying problems are **as hard as those on modular arithmetic**, but every operation the attacker must do is **more computationally demanding**, so legitimate users can:

- use **much shorter keys (~1/10 the length)**;
- run **much faster**.

Applications: **ECDSA** (digital signatures) and **ECDH** (key agreement).

blocks.caption: Example elliptic curve; point addition R = P + Q is defined by the chord-and-tangent (reflection) rule.

blocks.text: Key derivation functions (KDF)

KDFs were originally based on cryptographic hashes:

- **PBKDF2** (RFC-2898) uses **SHA-1**, with salt $|S| \geq 64$ and iteration count $\geq 1000$.
- **HKDF** (RFC-5869) uses **HMAC**.
Quantum computers and post-quantum cryptography

DH security reduces to the **discrete logarithm**: if $A = g^{X} \bmod p$ then $X = \log_g A \bmod p$. The number of multiplications grows **exponentially** with the number of bits of $p$. More efficient classical algorithms exist (e.g. **Pohlig-Hellman**, the **number field sieve**) but they remain **exponential**. Crucially, solving the discrete logarithm polynomially would let you factorise polynomially too (and vice-versa) — so RSA and DH are equally threatened by a fast factoring/log algorithm.

Proposed by **Peter Shor** to **factorise integers**. It runs **very fast on quantum computers** — logarithmic complexity, about $O((\log N)^3)$ for a number $N$. Quantum computers able to run Shor on non-trivial numbers do **not yet exist** (many qubits are needed just for error correction). Milestones from the slides:

- **2012:** a 7-qubit computer solved $21 = 3 \times 7$.
- **2014:** adiabatic quantum computation factored $56153 = 233 \times 241$.
- **2023:** IBM **Osprey**, 443 quantum bits.

If such a machine arrives, **RSA, DSA, and ECC become immediately weak** — hence the threat "harvest now, decrypt later."

PQC urgently needs **new hard problems that are also exponential on quantum computers**. The slides list **6 approaches**:

- **Lattice-based** cryptography
- **Multivariate** cryptography
- **Hash-based** cryptography
- **Code-based** cryptography
- **Isogeny-based** cryptography
- **Symmetric-key quantum resistance**

NIST's finalized post-quantum standards:

- **CRYSTALS-Kyber → ML-KEM** (Module-Lattice-Based Key-Encapsulation Mechanism);
- **CRYSTALS-Dilithium → ML-DSA** (Module-Lattice-Based Digital Signature Algorithm);
- **SPHINCS+ → SLH-DSA** (Stateless Hash-Based Digital Signature Algorithm — a backup in case lattice methods are found weak).

blocks.title: Symmetric crypto vs. quantum
blocks.text: Symmetric cryptography and hash functions are, as far as we know today, **not much affected** by quantum computers. **Grover's algorithm** only **doubles the speed** of attacks against known algorithms, which is fixed simply by **doubling the key length**. So no radically new technology is needed for symmetric crypto — engineering and incremental updates suffice.
Certificates and PKI

Public-key crypto works only if the private key stays secret **and** the public key is genuinely linked to its owner. The open question: who guarantees that a given public key really belongs to the claimed person? Distributing keys out-of-band does not scale. The solution is **digital certificates**.

A certificate is "a data structure used to securely bind a public key to some attributes" — typically an identity (but also email, DNS, IP, etc.). It is **digitally signed by the issuer**, called the **Certification Authority (CA)**, has a **limited lifetime**, and can be **revoked**. By verifying the CA's signature, anyone can trust the key-to-identity binding without meeting the owner.

The **technical and administrative infrastructure** for creating, distributing, and revoking public-key certificates. To verify a certificate signed by CA1 you need CA1's certificate (signed by CA2), and so on up a **hierarchy** to a trusted **Root CA** (Top-Level CA). Revocation is published via **CRL** (Certificate Revocation List) or **OCSP** (Online Certificate Status Protocol); the relying party must check a certificate was valid at signing time.

steps.label: Recompute and compare
steps.text: Hash the data to get $digest(data)$; decrypt the signature with the signer's public key to get the embedded $digest$; the signature is valid iff the two match.

steps.label: Trust the public key via its certificate
steps.text: The signer's public key $K_{PUB}(X)$ comes from a certificate (subject: X) that is itself signed by a CA.

steps.label: Walk the chain
steps.text: To trust a certificate signed by CA1 you need CA1's certificate (signed by CA2), which needs CA2's certificate (signed by CA3), and so on — requiring a (hierarchical) certification and distribution infrastructure.

blocks.text: Certificates form a **tree** rooted at a **Top-Level CA (TLCA)**, also called the **(Trusted) Root CA**. For example: a root **CA EU** signs the certificates of **CA IT** and **CA ES**; **CA IT** in turn signs **CA Torino** and **CA Milano**. Trust flows downward from the root; you only need to trust the root to validate any leaf certificate via the chain.

Any certificate can be **revoked before its expiration date**, either on request by the **owner (subject)** or autonomously by the **issuer**. When verifying a signature, the receiver must check that the certificate was **valid at signature time**; this check is the responsibility of the **relying party (RP)**.

Two revocation mechanisms:

- **CRL (Certificate Revocation List):** a list of revoked certificates, signed by the CA or a delegated party.
- **OCSP (Online Certificate Status Protocol):** a server answers queries about a certificate's status, and its responses must be **signed by the server**.
Secure electronic documents

There are three ways to package a signature with the document:

- **Enveloping signature:** the signature contains the document (the signed data wraps the document inside). Example: **PKCS#7**.
- **Enveloped signature:** the signature is embedded inside the document. Example: **PDF**.
- **Detached signature:** the signature is a separate file alongside the document. Example: **PKCS#7**.

A document can carry more than one signature:

- **Parallel / independent signatures:** each signer $X, Y, Z$ signs the **same original document** independently; the signatures sit side by side and do not depend on each other.
- **Sequential / hierarchical signatures:** signer $Y$ signs $X$'s already-signed document, and $Z$ signs that — each signature covers the previous signatures too (e.g. $ds(doc,X)$, then $ds(-,Y)$, then $ds(-,Z)$), establishing an order.

A **time-stamp** is evidence that some data **existed before a certain date**. It is issued by a **TSA (Time-Stamping Authority)** under **RFC-3161**, which defines:

- the **request protocol — TSP (Time-Stamp Protocol)**;
- the **format — TST (Time-Stamp Token)**.

The client hashes the data and sends the digest to the TSA; the TSA returns a **TST** containing the digest, the **date and time**, and the **TSA's digital signature**.

A **QES** is a digital signature that (a) uses a **Qualified Certificate** and (b) is generated with **ad-hoc secure signature-creation devices**. It is designed to meet **legal requirements** — to be as good as a handwritten signature and usable **in court** — and is time-stamped by **trusted TSAs**. The keys come from a **Qualified Certificate** that includes attributes clarifying the **purpose and scope** of the key (e.g. that it is for digital signatures), issued by **trusted CAs recognised by the individual states**.

Standardised signature formats (ETSI):

- **CAdES** — CMS Advanced Electronic Signatures (ETSI TS 101 733/734).
- **XAdES** — XML Advanced Electronic Signatures (ETSI TS 101 903/904, based on XML-dsig).
- **PAdES** — PDF Advanced Electronic Signature Profiles, ISO-32000 / PDF format (ETSI TS 102 778).
- **ASiC (Associated Signature Containers)** — a ZIP-based container associating documents with **detached** CAdES/XAdES signatures and time-stamps (ETSI TS 119 162).

Underlying standards include **CMS (Cryptographic Message Syntax)** and **ESS (Enhanced Security Services)**.

blocks.title: The big picture
blocks.text:
- **Symmetric** crypto = fast, bulk encryption, but the key-sharing problem.
- **Asymmetric** crypto = solves key distribution and signing, but slow, so use it to wrap a symmetric key (hybrid).
- **Hashes** = integrity; **MACs** = integrity + authentication with a shared secret; **signatures** = data authentication + integrity with a private key.
- **Certificates + PKI** = the trust layer that ties public keys to real identities.
- Security always rests on the **key**, never on a secret algorithm (Kerckhoffs).
